symptoms.symptom_name: Rich autonomic prodrome (nausea, profuse diaphoresis, bodily warmth, visual 'tunnelling', tinnitus/roaring) before loss of consciousness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A gradual, rich autonomic prodrome (nausea, diaphoresis, visual tunnelling, tinnitus) is characteristic of neurally mediated reflex syncope and is one of the strongest discriminators from cardiac syncope.
Cardiac syncope is typically sudden and unheralded; a clear rich prodrome argues against it.
An autonomic (rather than epileptic aura) prodrome and the subsequent rapid recovery favour syncope over seizure.

symptoms.symptom_name: Emotional/situational trigger: sight of blood/surgery on TV while standing in a warm room, in a squeamish individual

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: An emotional visual trigger (blood/surgery) in a warm environment is a classic precipitant of the vasovagal reflex, especially in a blood-injury-phobic individual.
Orthostatic hypotension characteristically causes symptoms immediately on rising, not in response to an emotional visual trigger.

symptoms.symptom_name: Rapid, complete, spontaneous recovery within ~20 seconds of lying flat, with no postictal confusion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Rapid full recovery once supine, without prolonged confusion, is typical of reflex syncope as cerebral perfusion is quickly restored by lying flat.
A prolonged postictal confusional state is expected after a generalised seizure; its absence with immediate orientation argues strongly against epilepsy.

symptoms.symptom_name: Bystander-observed marked pallor and a limp (atonic) fall during the episode

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Profound pallor and loss of postural tone (a limp fall) reflect the reflex vasodilatation/bradycardia and hypoperfusion of a vasovagal faint.
Marked pallor with a limp fall is more typical of syncope than of a seizure (which more often involves stiffening/jerking and cyanosis/flushing).

symptoms.symptom_name: No exertional or no-warning onset, no palpitations, no chest pain

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Exertional/unheralded onset, palpitations and chest pain are the cardinal features of dangerous cardiac syncope; their absence substantially lowers its probability.
A warned, non-exertional faint without palpitations is consistent with a benign reflex mechanism.

symptoms.symptom_name: No tongue-biting, no incontinence, and no tonic-clonic movements

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Lateral tongue-biting, incontinence and tonic-clonic movements favour a generalised seizure; their absence supports syncope over epilepsy.

symptoms.symptom_name: No family history of sudden or unexplained death at a young age

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of a family history of premature sudden death lowers the pre-test probability of an inheritable arrhythmia/cardiomyopathy syndrome as the cause of a first syncope.

symptoms.symptom_name: Fell onto carpet with no injury detected or felt (no head strike)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of injury from the fall does not alter the diagnosis but removes the mechanical complication (a head strike) that would otherwise raise the triage tier and mandate same-day assessment.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal neurological examination, negative active-stand orthostatic vitals, no cardiac murmur, and no head trauma (clinician-measured)

investigation_details.investigation_name: In-person neurological and cardiovascular examination with active-stand orthostatic vitals and head/injury assessment
investigation_details.expected_result: Cranial nerves II-XII intact, normal power/sensation/coordination, no nystagmus, no tongue bite, equal reactive pupils; regular rate and rhythm with dual heart sounds and no murmur; no significant postural BP drop on active-stand testing; no palpable head trauma or laceration from the fall onto carpet
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Without in-person examination, a focal neurological deficit, a significant orthostatic drop, a murmur of structural heart disease, or an occult injury from the fall cannot be formally excluded.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A normal neurological and cardiac examination with negative orthostatic vitals and no head trauma supports an uncomplicated benign reflex faint.

symptoms.symptom_name: Baseline resting 12-lead ECG (to exclude an occult arrhythmic substrate)

investigation_details.expected_result: Expected to be normal in benign vasovagal syncope; screens for occult substrates (prolonged QT, pre-excitation/WPW, Brugada pattern, conduction disease) that would reclassify the syncope as potentially arrhythmic
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: An occult arrhythmic substrate presenting as a first syncope could be missed if no baseline ECG is obtained.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A baseline 12-lead ECG is the standard low-yield screen recommended for any first syncope to exclude an occult arrhythmic cause; a normal ECG further supports the benign diagnosis.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: The combination of a rich autonomic prodrome, an emotional blood/surgery visual trigger in a warm room, bystander pallor with a limp fall, and rapid complete recovery on lying flat without postictal confusion is a high-specificity constellation for benign reflex syncope, especially when the dangerous-syncope screen (exertional/no-warning onset, palpitations, chest pain, family history of sudden death) is negative.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Resting 12-lead ECG (routine, as part of GP review)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Baseline low-yield screen for an occult arrhythmic substrate (long QT, pre-excitation, Brugada) recommended for any first syncope; expected normal here.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person neurological and cardiovascular examination with active-stand orthostatic vitals (routine GP review)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Confirms a normal neurological and cardiac examination, excludes a significant postural BP drop and a murmur of structural heart disease, and confirms no injury from the fall - completing reassurance for a first faint.
Not indicated for a classic, uncomplicated first episode of vasovagal syncope; reserved for recurrent or diagnostically uncertain cases.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Emergency ambulance / ED work-up for a fully recovered, red-flag-negative reflex faint
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Unnecessary over-triage for a recovered young adult with a classic emotionally triggered reflex faint and no red flags; reassurance with routine GP review and a baseline ECG is appropriate, reserving emergency care for dangerous-syncope or delayed head-injury red flags.
